speaker: interviewer
text: 9 of October 1995. Henry Kinsley. Tape number five. You were saying that you came to New York and you worked for S. Klein.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in department store, S. Klein, picking dresses from the floor, you know? The customers came and when they said to hang up. She dropped it. This was my job. I didn't talk English. A few words, the dirty ones. And after eight months, I got another job in a knitwear house, wholesale, where I learned little bit about knitwear. I stayed over there with them for three years, became-- became a floor supervisor. And then I left them, because I didn't see future over there. And I wind up with a-- a knitwear, wholesale place on Broadway, and I work with this guy for 30 years, running the whole show, production, everything, until I retired. After retirement, I worked for American Express part-time, you know, just keep myself busy. Meantime, my wife passed away. My kids got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two, a daughter and son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren-- each has a couple, so it's four. And I work for American Express for about three years, part-time. Then I switched to Philip Morris, part-time. And then I work a bank near where I live, in neighborhood another three years. And then I said, that's enough. I liquidate everything in New York. And my daughter came here with her husband, bought this, says, Daddy, this is for you. And that's why I wind up here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I-- can I just ask you one more thing before we close? Why do you think that Schindler did not go with the rest of the Germans when they cleared out of the camp, out of Brunnlitz? Why do you think Schindler stayed behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the German were everywhere. They were surrendering, the Germans. So where are you going, with them or to whom? And here, he got the Jews, who's going to protect him, who's going to take him out. That's what they did. They took him from the camp. They brought him over there to Germany over there, which he's got a lot of problems, too, because they find out that he helped the Jews in Germany. That's what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I never got contact with him after the war. He used to travel. Came to New York, visit this, you know? He-- he know what he's doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they came. They want him to go with them. I remember one day after the war came two SS men with guns. We got scared again. Oh, they're coming back. They told him, hey, listen. You better go. He wouldn't go because he is protection for the Jews. That's why he worked for them, to save him, and they saved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, is there anything that you would like to tell me that I haven't asked you or that we haven't talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I tell you? Everything is unpleasant. I tried to forget about things, which is not easy. But you have to continue to live and go with your life. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your wife ever speak about your experiences to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They asked. They asked a few times. They watch the-- all the shows, this. But I-- very seldom. I figure, what the-- I would spare them. They know. They read the books. They got the tapes. But I never-- once we were sitting, but we couldn't continue. We were sniffing and tissues. I says, enough is enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any message for future generations that can live beyond us? What-- a message about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen. I hope this helps. I mean, doesn't happen again. And just hope that-- that-- that there's not going to be something like this again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one solution I got for them. You got a land, which is Israel, support it because whatever happened to you, you got where to go. We couldn't go any place. Nobody was let us. You know what I mean? So support, help, because this is the only country which you can count on. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Henry Kinsley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on. Hang on. Just watch me, and I'll tell you. I'll go like this when you should-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Kinsley, my friend Richard Abrahamer. And this picture was taken in Queens, in 1950. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jackie Silberberg, her mother, Elenora Silberberg, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Laura Kinsley, my son's daughter, And Danny Kinsley and Henry Kinsley.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken right over here in Delray Beach in my apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last year, 1994. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-in-law, Joseph Silberberg, his daughter, Jackie Silberberg, and his wife, my daughter, Elenora Silberberg. This picture was taken this year, summertime, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Joseph Kinsley. This picture was taken after he graduated from high school, in school somewhere in Queens. The year, I don't remember.
